pair_convention.spot_explanation: SGD/JPY at 123.70 means 1 SGD buys 123.70 JPY. A rising pair means SGD is strengthening against JPY (each SGD buys more yen); a falling pair means SGD is weakening against JPY (each SGD buys fewer yen, i.e., JPY is strengthening vs SGD).
pair_convention.bearish_means: pair FALLS (SGD weakens vs JPY, i.e., JPY strengthens vs SGD)
pair_convention.quote_dossier_tactical_stance: Bullish JPY (tactical 3/5, strategic 3/5, meaning JPY strengthening = USD/JPY falling)

executive_summary: SGD/JPY trades at 123.70 (9 May close), up approximately 7.3% over the past year and sitting at the 90th percentile of its 52-week range (107.08–125.47). The pair rallied sharply from the April 2025 tariff-shock low of 107.08 through a steady uptrend driven by MAS tightening, BOJ caution, and Singapore's AI-electronics export boom, before correcting approximately 2 yen from the late-April 2026 highs near 125.47. The dominant bilateral narrative is a tug-of-war between Singapore's structural superiority (AAA, 17.5% C/A surplus, MAS slope steepening) and Japan's incipient rate normalisation (6-3 hawkish BOJ vote, 2.8% FY2026 CPI projection, JGB 10Y at 2.50%). The bilateral policy rate differential stands at only +40 bps (SORA 1.15% vs BOJ 0.75%), and the 10Y yield spread has inverted (SGS 2.10% below JGB 2.50%), signalling that bond markets have already begun pricing BOJ normalisation dominance. Tactically, the BOJ's June 17 meeting is the single most important binary event — a hike would compress SGD/JPY by 200–300 pips, while a hold sustains the current range. Our headline stance is mildly Neutral-to-Bullish SGD/JPY tactically (conviction 2/5, pair stays supported near 122.50–125.00) and Bearish strategically (conviction 2/5, target 119–122 by November 2026), as the BOJ normalisation path is expected to erode the carry-and-growth differential that has propelled SGD/JPY higher over the past twelve months.

market_context.valuation_assessment: SGD is approximately 34% undervalued vs JPY on OECD PPP terms (implied fair value ~SGD/JPY 80 vs spot 123.70). JPY's BIS REER sits in the bottom decile of post-1990 history (~8th percentile), while SGD's BIS REER at ~117 is in the 88th percentile. The PPP gap is extreme but structurally embedded — it has persisted for years and does not generate tactical signal. However, the magnitude of JPY undervaluation (40% vs USD, 34% vs SGD) anchors the strategic case for JPY mean-reversion and gradual SGD/JPY depreciation.

positioning.net_pair_implication: The combined implied positioning for SGD/JPY is moderately long — the street is long both SGD and JPY vs USD, but since both currencies are expected to appreciate vs USD, the cross-rate signal is muted. There is no extreme crowd positioning in this cross, and positioning is not generating directional alpha for SGD/JPY.
positioning.crowding_assessment: Balanced — no extreme crowd positioning in the cross

pair_technicals.key_moving_averages: 50d SMA: 124.37 (overhead resistance); 100d SMA: 123.68 (acting as support near spot); 200d SMA: 121.91 (strategic support). MA configuration is bullish-stacked on a strategic basis (spot above 100d above 200d), though the 50d SMA overhead suggests near-term consolidation.
pair_technicals.chart_pattern: Post-breakout consolidation — pair has successfully cleared historical resistance levels to establish a fresh structural peak at 125.47, then corrected sharply on 30 April BOJ hawkish vote. Now consolidating around the 100d SMA in a 122.10–123.99 range. RSI ~60 (neutral); MACD 0.4567 (buy signal). The dominant regime is transitioning from a strong uptrend to range-bound consolidation ahead of the BOJ June decision.

pair_volatility.rv_vs_iv: Realised vol broadly in line with implied; no significant variance risk premium. SGD/JPY vol is a derivative of USD/SGD (~5% 1M IV) and USD/JPY (~9.5% 1M IV), dampened by SGD's managed float.
pair_volatility.risk_reversals: Not directly quoted in OTC markets (thin cross). The JPY leg dominates vol dynamics, with JPY-call premium (USD/JPY put bid) post-MoF intervention on 1 May 2026.
pair_volatility.vol_term_structure: Modest backwardation likely through the June BOJ and FOMC meetings (both 16–17 June), with event risk priced in the front end of the curve.
pair_volatility.vol_regime: Low-moderate vol / consolidating — TradingView reports SGD/JPY volatility rating at 1.63% short-term; daily ATR ~0.80 yen (~65 pips)

market_context.recent_regime: SGD/JPY rallied steadily from the 107.08 low in April 2025 through an ascending channel, reaching the 125.47 high in late April 2026 — a gain of approximately 18.4 yen (+17%) over twelve months. The pair then corrected sharply on 30 April, falling from 125.47 to 123.02 intraday (–2.4 yen) as the BOJ's 6-3 hawkish vote and MoF's ¥5.4T intervention in USD/JPY transmitted yen strength across all crosses. Since May 1, the pair has stabilised in a 122.10–123.99 range, consolidating around the 100d SMA. The dominant regime is transitioning from a strong uptrend to a range-bound consolidation as the market awaits the BOJ June decision.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move approximately 4 pips over 30 days before carry is erased; easily overwhelmed by any single event-driven move

forward_points.1m: ~flat to slightly positive (approximately 0 to +3 pips, reflecting minimal +40 bps rate differential)
forward_points.3m: ~−10 pips or less (negligible forward-implied SGD/JPY depreciation)
forward_points.6m: ~−25 pips (forward curve approximately flat, implying forward-implied rate ~123.45)

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The bilateral policy rate differential is SGD (SORA 3M ~1.15%) minus JPY (BOJ 0.75%) = +40 bps annualised, the narrowest among all SGD crosses against G10 currencies. The carry-to-vol ratio at +40 bps divided by ~7% pair vol equals 0.06 — well below the 0.30+ threshold where carry 'works' as a systematic return driver. The carry is essentially immaterial for positioning decisions. Forward points at 1M are approximately flat to slightly positive; at 3M forward-implied depreciation is negligible (<10 pips); at 6M the forward curve is approximately flat (~123.45). The carry regime is 'dead money' — neither carry nor cost of carry is a meaningful factor for this pair. The breakeven move over 30 days is approximately 4 pips, easily overwhelmed by any single event-driven move. Critically, the carry regime is in transition: if BOJ hikes to 1.00% at the June MPM, the differential flips to −25 bps, converting SGD/JPY to a negative-carry pair for the first time — a structural regime shift that would be bearish for sustained SGD/JPY positioning.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The MAS-BOJ policy rate differential stands at approximately +40 bps (SORA 1.15% vs BOJ 0.75%), the narrowest among all SGD crosses against G10 currencies. MAS tightened the S$NEER slope on 14 April 2026, while BOJ held with a 6-3 hawkish split on 28 April — both central banks are in tightening mode, which creates an offsetting dynamic for this pair. The rate trajectory over the next 2–4 weeks is indeterminate: MAS has no scheduled action until the July MPS, while the BOJ's Summary of Opinions (released ~7 May) and the June 17 MPM are the key inflection points. If the BOJ delivers a surprise June hike to 1.00%, the rate differential flips to −25 bps (SGD/JPY becomes negative carry), which would be structurally bearish for the pair. Sovereign yield differentials reinforce the ambiguity: SGS 10Y at 2.10% is now 40 bps below JGB 10Y at 2.50% — a notable inversion where the AAA-rated Singapore paper yields less than Japan's A+ paper, reflecting BOJ normalisation repricing.
key_bilateral_metrics: MAS SORA 3M: ~1.15% vs BOJ policy rate: 0.75% = +40 bps differential
SGS 10Y: 2.10% vs JGB 10Y: 2.50% = −40 bps (SGD yields below JPY)
BOJ June hike probability: ~35–40% (three April dissenters at 1.00%)
MAS July tightening probability: ~25–35%
Annualised carry: +40 bps (negligible)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices continued range-trading in the 122–125 zone, reflecting roughly equal MAS and BOJ hawkish credibility. A BOJ June surprise hike is under-priced at ~35% vs our estimate of ~40–45%.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the rate-differential trajectory decisively favours JPY. MUFG projects BOJ reaching 1.00% by Q2/Q3 2026 and 1.25% by Q4 — at which point the SGD-JPY rate gap would be −10 to −110 bps, with JPY yielding more than SGD. This is a regime shift: SGD/JPY has historically been a positive-carry pair; the BOJ normalisation path converts it to negative carry by year-end. MAS, conversely, operates through the S$NEER band slope rather than a policy rate, with SORA passively tracking USD funding conditions. As Fed funds compress under Warsh (one cut expected by Q4 2026), SORA drifts lower, further narrowing and eventually inverting the SGD-JPY differential. The 10Y sovereign yield inversion (SGS 2.10% < JGB 2.50%) is a strategic signal: bond markets have already priced BOJ normalisation dominance, and the FX rate typically follows the rate differential with a 3–6 month lag.
key_bilateral_metrics: Projected BOJ rate end-2026: 1.00–1.25% (MUFG/Sony)
Projected SORA 3M end-2026: ~0.90–1.10% (drifting lower with Fed)
Projected end-2026 SGD-JPY policy gap: −10 to −110 bps (inverted)
SGS 10Y vs JGB 10Y: currently −40 bps, expected to widen to −60 to −80 bps
Bloomberg consensus USD/JPY end-2026: 153 (implies SGD/JPY ~121 at USD/SGD 1.25)
strategic.what_is_priced: The spot at 123.70 is above the level implied by end-2026 consensus (SGD/JPY ~121). The pair is trading rich to the rate-differential trajectory, creating a strategic bearish edge.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (55) vs Strategic Bearish (60): the 2–4 week window lacks a decisive catalyst (BOJ holds until June 17), while the 1–6 month window encompasses 2–3 BOJ MPMs where hike delivery would structurally compress the pair.

carry_analysis.breakeven_move: ~4 pips over 30 days, easily overwhelmed by any single event-driven move
carry_analysis.regime_transition_risk: If BOJ hikes to 1.00% at June MPM, differential flips to −25 bps, converting pair to negative carry for the first time — a structural bearish regime shift

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Singapore's growth differential over Japan is substantial: Q1 2026 GDP at +4.6% y/y vs Japan's projected ~0.5–1.0%. Singapore's unemployment at 2.1% matches Japan's 2.7% in tightness, but the growth rate is 3–4x higher, driven by the AI-electronics export boom (NODX +15.3% y/y, electronics +74% y/y). Japan's April PMI at 52.4 vs Singapore's 50.7 shows converging activity levels, but the quality of growth differs — Singapore's is export-led and tech-driven, while Japan faces a stagflationary mix (BOJ FY2026 core CPI at 2.8% but GDP at 0.5%). Inflation dynamics favour SGD/JPY upside tactically: Singapore's core CPI at 1.7% is well-managed by MAS's appreciation framework, while Japan's cost-push inflation (energy subsidies masking 2.4% core-core) creates policy uncertainty rather than clean currency strength. The growth-differential supports continued demand for SGD as the higher-growth currency in the immediate 2–4 week window.
key_bilateral_metrics: Singapore GDP Q1 2026: +4.6% y/y vs Japan GDP FY2026 projection: 0.5%
Singapore core CPI: 1.7% vs Japan core-core: 2.4%
Singapore NODX: +15.3% y/y vs Japan exports: +11.7% y/y
Manufacturing PMI: Singapore 50.7 vs Japan 54.9 (Japan manufacturing stronger)
Shunto 2026: 5.09% (Japan wage anchor for inflation persistence)
tactical.what_is_priced: The growth differential is well-priced at current levels. The tactical upside comes from the upcoming Singapore April CPI release (25 May) which could surprise to the upside, reinforcing MAS hawkishness before the BOJ moves.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, Japan's wage-inflation cycle (Shunto 5.09%, BOJ FY2026 core CPI at 2.8%) creates a durable normalisation impulse that is structurally JPY-bullish. Singapore's growth advantage persists but at decelerating pace (Q1 GDP −0.3% q/q SAAR), and the AI-electronics tailwind faces cyclical risk if global semi demand softens in H2 2026. The growth differential narrows strategically as Japan benefits from Takaichi fiscal stimulus (¥21.3T supplementary + ¥120T budget) while Singapore's export-dependent model faces US tariff risk and China demand uncertainty.
key_bilateral_metrics: Japan FY2026 GDP BOJ projection: 0.5% vs Singapore MTI 2026: 2.0–4.0%
Japan Shunto wage persistence: anchors CPI above 2% through 2026
Singapore NODX cyclical risk: AI-electronics +74% y/y unlikely sustainable
Japan retail sales: +1.7% y/y (resilient domestic demand)
Singapore tourist arrivals Q1: 4.4M (services offset manufacturing softening)
strategic.what_is_priced: The growth differential narrowing is partially priced through the JGB yield rise (10Y at 2.50% vs SGS 2.10%), but not yet fully transmitted to SGD/JPY spot.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bullish (65) reflecting the immediate growth-data advantage; Strategic Neutral (55) reflecting the closing gap as Japan's wage-inflation cycle generates BOJ action.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: SGD/JPY has moderate risk-proxy sensitivity through the JPY safe-haven leg; boosted by +0.03 tactical and +0.02 strategic

tactical.bilateral_analysis: SGD/JPY is a moderate-risk-sensitivity cross pair. It does not have the pure risk-proxy character of AUD/JPY (which has ~0.7 correlation to global equities) but retains meaningful sensitivity through the JPY safe-haven leg. VIX at 17 sits in mid-range territory; neither risk-on euphoria nor risk-off panic dominates. The Iran ceasefire fragility is the key bilateral risk vector: a ceasefire failure would spike oil, hurting both Singapore (net importer) and Japan (95.9% ME crude dependence), but JPY would likely strengthen on safe-haven flows despite the terms-of-trade hit — net SGD/JPY bearish. Conversely, ceasefire formalisation benefits Singapore's terms of trade more than Japan's (Singapore's refining margins offset crude costs, while Japan is a pure net loser from high oil). The STI near record highs and Nikkei at 59,500 reflect calm equity markets in both economies. The risk regime is balanced for this pair.
key_bilateral_metrics: VIX: 17.1 (35th percentile 1Y)
STI: 4,941 (near all-time high)
SGD/JPY correlation to VIX: moderately negative (~−0.2 to −0.3)
Brent: $102/bbl (key bilateral risk barometer)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price benign risk-on (low VIX, equity records). The unpriced risk is asymmetric Iran re-escalation, which would be net SGD/JPY-bearish via the JPY safe-haven reflex.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, if oil normalises toward $80 as Iran ceasefire firms, JPY's classical safe-haven correlation re-asserts, and risk-off episodes (US recession scare at ~30% probability) would favour JPY over SGD. Japan's $3.3T NIIP underpins structural repatriation flows during global stress. SGD benefits from the Asian safe-haven role within ASEAN but lacks the global reserve-currency repatriation channel that JPY possesses.
key_bilateral_metrics: Japan NIIP: +$3.3T (world's largest, structural repatriation anchor)
Singapore MAS reserves: USD 427.3bn (large but not NIIP-equivalent)
Probability of risk-off episode in 6M: ~30%
JPY safe-haven beta: re-asserts post-oil-normalisation
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets under-price the strategic JPY safe-haven re-assertion once the energy overlay fades.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (55) reflecting balanced current conditions; Strategic Bearish (50) reflecting JPY's structural safe-haven advantage over 1–6 months.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Neither leg provides a clear tactical positioning signal. SGD has no CFTC data; JPY leveraged funds are net long +56,400 contracts (75th percentile) — an atypical configuration. The combined implied positioning for SGD/JPY is moderately long (street is long both SGD and JPY vs USD, but the cross-rate signal is ambiguous). MAS reserves accumulation (+USD 8bn in April) signals MAS leaning against SGD strength, while the MoF's ¥5.4T intervention caps JPY weakness. Both central banks are actively managing their respective currencies, creating a dampened flow dynamic in the cross. Singapore imports from Japan totalled US$22.22 billion in 2024, while Japan exported US$19.82 billion to Singapore in 2024, indicating roughly balanced bilateral trade flows with no dominant unidirectional FX conversion pressure.
key_bilateral_metrics: CFTC JPY leveraged net long: +56,400 contracts (75th percentile)
CFTC SGD: zero open interest (uninformative)
MAS reserves: +USD 8bn in April (intervention signal)
MoF intervention: ¥5.4T on 1 May (intervention signal for JPY)
Bilateral trade: roughly balanced (~$20bn each way)
tactical.what_is_priced: Positioning is not generating directional alpha for SGD/JPY. The pair is trading on macro fundamentals, not flow dynamics.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the flow picture favours JPY through structural repatriation channels. Singapore has emerged as Asia's largest source of inward FDI stock to Japan at 0.8 trillion yen. Japanese firms operate over 1,000 subsidiaries in Singapore, while Singapore's sovereign wealth funds, such as GIC and Temasek, have channelled billions into Japanese equities. These bidirectional investment flows create natural hedging demand, but the net flow direction favours JPY appreciation as Japanese institutional foreign-bond repatriation accelerates when USD/JPY falls below 150 (hedging cost improvement). Singapore's family-office capital inflows provide durable SGD support but are structural and already embedded in the current rate.
key_bilateral_metrics: Japanese institutional foreign-bond demand: paused at USD/JPY 156+ (too expensive to hedge)
GIC/Temasek Japan allocations: billions (structural, not tactical)
Japan GPIF: ~$1.6T AUM, quarterly hedge-ratio adjustments
Singapore family office SGD demand: continuing structural support
strategic.what_is_priced: The structural repatriation channel is partially priced in JPY but under-priced in SGD/JPY. The cross lacks the attention of major flow desks.

factors.horizon_split: Neutral tactically and Bearish strategically — the flow dynamics require BOJ action to activate the repatriation channel, which is a strategic phenomenon.

tactical.combined_positioning_signal: Moderately long SGD/JPY implied — both SGD and JPY are consensus longs vs USD, but the cross signal is muted. No extreme positioning in this cross.
tactical.unwind_trigger: BOJ disappoints in June with a dovish hold — the +56,400 leveraged long JPY would partially unwind, lifting SGD/JPY by ~100–200 pips
tactical.historical_analogue: Post-2023 BOJ YCC tweak: JPY longs built aggressively then partially unwound when BOJ pace disappointed, causing SGD/JPY to recover 150–200 pips before the next leg lower
tactical.contrarian_case: The contrarian risk is a JPY-long unwind: if BOJ disappoints in June (dovish hold), the +56,400 leveraged long JPY would partially unwind, lifting SGD/JPY by ~100–200 pips. The magnitude would be moderate given the JPY long is not at extreme levels (+100k threshold). Conversely, there are no SGD-specific longs to squeeze, making the contrarian scenario asymmetrically JPY-unwind-driven.

strategic.combined_positioning_signal: Modestly stretched long JPY but not extreme. If BOJ delays normalisation beyond September, a positioning-driven SGD/JPY rally to 126+ is possible.
strategic.unwind_trigger: BOJ delays normalisation beyond September 2026 AND hawkish dissent count drops — JPY longs forced to capitulate
strategic.historical_analogue: 2024 early-year JPY long squeeze: leveraged funds built JPY longs on BOJ normalisation hopes, unwound sharply when BOJ remained cautious, driving USD/JPY from 142 back to 155 and SGD/JPY higher
strategic.contrarian_case: Strategic positioning is modestly stretched long JPY but not extreme. If BOJ delays normalisation beyond September, a positioning-driven SGD/JPY rally to 126+ is possible with ~20% probability. The contrarian trade is to fade the BOJ normalisation consensus if data disappoints — SGD/JPY would benefit from structural growth and MAS credibility differentials in the absence of BOJ delivery.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Oil-price differential is the dominant bilateral factor for this pair; boosted by +0.02 on both horizons

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The net commodity exposure for this pair is clearly SGD-bullish. Both Singapore and Japan are major net energy importers, so the oil shock hurts both — but Japan's vulnerability is vastly greater: Japan imports 95.9% of crude from the Middle East vs Singapore's more diversified mix that includes refining margin benefits. At Brent $102: Singapore's terms of trade are modestly negative (crude import cost up) but partially offset by elevated refining crack spreads and LNG re-export margins; Japan's terms of trade are severely negative with no domestic production offset. Using the framework's commodity direction rules for oil up: SGD gets mild positive (entrepôt refining margins) while JPY gets strong negative (95.9% ME dependence). Net: SGD/JPY rises (bullish) during elevated oil. The tactical oil level ($102) is still 35% above pre-war, maintaining the SGD/JPY bullish external-balance impulse.
key_bilateral_metrics: Japan ME crude dependence: 95.9% vs Singapore: diversified net importer with refining offset
Singapore C/A: 17.5% of GDP vs Japan C/A: ~4% of GDP
Singapore NODX: +15.3% y/y (AI-electronics export boom)
Japan trade balance March: ¥+667bn (miss vs +1,106bn consensus)
Brent: $102/bbl (elevated, benefits SGD/JPY long)
tactical.what_is_priced: The oil-shock asymmetry is reflected in the ~18 yen SGD/JPY rally from the 2025 low. Further upside from this factor is limited unless Brent re-spikes above $115.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: If Iran ceasefire holds and Brent reverts to $80–90, Japan's terms of trade improve significantly, removing the SGD/JPY bullish external-balance tailwind. Japan's $3.3T NIIP and primary income surplus (~¥45–50T annualised) provide structural support that dominates the cyclical trade-balance damage once energy normalises. Singapore's 17.5% C/A surplus is structurally larger, but the differential narrows as Japan's terms of trade recover. The AI-electronics tailwind (NODX +74% y/y in electronics) remains Singapore-positive but faces cyclical moderation. Net: the bilateral external-balance factor becomes neutral as oil normalises.
key_bilateral_metrics: Projected Brent Q3 2026: $80–90 (if ceasefire holds)
Japan primary income surplus: ~¥45–50T annualised (structural)
Singapore C/A narrowing to ~15–16% of GDP on energy import costs
Japan C/A strengthening as oil declines
LNG (JKM) directional impact: strongly JPY-negative, mildly SGD-negative
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price a partial Brent normalisation. Our base case aligns with consensus; the edge is small.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bullish (60) reflecting current elevated oil; Strategic Neutral (55) reflecting the reversal as Brent normalises.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: SGD (Singapore): net energy importer but with significant refining/entrepôt offset — crude import costs partly recovered through elevated refining crack spreads and LNG re-export margins; net mild positive from high oil
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: JPY (Japan): pure net energy importer with 95.9% Middle East crude dependence, zero domestic production offset; net strongly negative from high oil
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Oil UP scenario: SGD receives mild positive (refining margins offset import costs) = +0.5 units; JPY receives strong negative (95.9% ME dependence, no offset) = −1.5 units. Net pair effect = SGD/JPY rises = Bullish. Oil DOWN scenario: SGD receives mild negative (refining margins compress) = −0.3 units; JPY receives strong positive (import costs fall sharply) = +1.5 units. Net pair effect = SGD/JPY falls = Bearish. At current Brent $102, the oil-elevated environment provides a persistent SGD/JPY bullish impulse through the asymmetric terms-of-trade channel.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Brent to ~$112: SGD/JPY bullish — Japan's import bill rises ~$15bn annualised with no offset; Singapore's refining margins expand further. Estimated +50 to +100 pips in SGD/JPY.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Brent to ~$92: SGD/JPY bearish — Japan's terms of trade improve significantly; Singapore loses refining premium. Estimated −50 to −80 pips in SGD/JPY.
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Brent crude — Japan's 95.9% Middle East dependence makes it the most oil-sensitive G10 currency, creating the dominant bilateral ToT asymmetry for this pair

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both MAS and BOJ have delivered credible hawkish communications. MAS tightened the S$NEER slope on 14 April with the strongest hawkish language since 2022; BOJ's 6-3 vote on 28 April with Ueda's 'real interest rates significantly low' language signals imminent normalisation. For the PAIR, these offsetting hawkish signals produce a neutral tactical stance — MAS hawkishness supports SGD while BOJ hawkishness supports JPY. The credibility premium is approximately equal: MAS has a 45-year track record of following through on policy signals, while BOJ under Ueda has become more transparent. The next communication events (BOJ Summary of Opinions ~7 May, Singapore April CPI 25 May) could tilt the balance but neither is expected to dramatically shift the bilateral guidance picture.
key_bilateral_metrics: MAS April tightening: slope steepened (delivered)
BOJ April vote: 6-3 hawkish (three dissents for 1.00%)
MAS July tightening probability: ~25–35%
BOJ June hike probability: ~35–40%
Credibility ranking: both top-tier within G10+SGD
tactical.what_is_priced: Both hawkish signals are priced; the market awaits delivery (BOJ June, MAS July).

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, the BOJ's guidance framework has more room to surprise hawkishly than MAS. The BOJ raised FY2026 core CPI to 2.8% — the largest upward revision in the price-stability-target era — and three board members already voted for 1.00%. MAS's guidance is well-established (slope steepened, inflation forecasts raised) but the next tightening is a lower-probability event (~25%). The asymmetry of guidance surprises favours JPY: a BOJ hike would compress SGD/JPY by 200–300 pips, while a further MAS tightening would lift SGD/JPY by only ~100 pips (because the rate-differential change is smaller and the S$NEER mechanism transmits gradually). The guidance divergence is not in direction (both hawkish) but in magnitude of remaining surprise.
key_bilateral_metrics: BOJ FY2026 core CPI projection: 2.8% (largest revision in history)
BOJ two-hike path probability: ~50–60% by year-end
MAS further tightening probability: ~25% at July
BOJ communication surprise frequency: 3–4 of last 10 meetings created 100 pip moves
MAS communication surprise frequency: very low (highly predictable)
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has priced one BOJ hike by Q3 but not two. The MAS stance is well-priced with low surprise potential.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (60) reflecting balanced hawkish credibility; Strategic Bearish (60) reflecting the asymmetry of remaining BOJ hawkish surprise potential.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The dominant bilateral event risk is the Iran ceasefire fragility, which affects both currencies asymmetrically (Japan more negatively from oil; Singapore moderately negatively). The BOJ June 17 MPM is the single most important bilateral catalyst within the 2–4 week extended window. PM Lawrence Wong and PM Takaichi Sanae upgraded Singapore-Japan relations to a Strategic Partnership on 18 March 2026, deepening cooperation across digital economy, semiconductors, and defence — a structurally positive bilateral development that reduces geopolitical friction. Two-way tourists between Japan and Singapore reached approximately 1.36 million in 2025, a meaningful bilateral services flow that transmits into FX markets.
key_bilateral_metrics: Iran ceasefire status: fragile, binary outcome
BOJ June 17 MPM: CRITICAL bilateral catalyst
Singapore-Japan Strategic Partnership: upgraded 18 March 2026
Bilateral tourism: 1.36M two-way in 2025
Singapore April CPI: 25 May (modest SGD catalyst)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing benign risk (VIX 17, equities at highs). The BOJ June binary is partially priced (~35–40% hike probability).

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Iran resolution paths (40% peace, 35% frozen, 25% re-escalation) affect SGD/JPY asymmetrically: peace is mildly SGD/JPY-bearish (JPY terms-of-trade recovery); re-escalation is ambiguously net-bearish (JPY safe-haven vs both-currencies-hurt). US-Japan trade risk (autos, semiconductors) is a Japan-specific headwind; US-Singapore trade risk is minimal. China demand is relevant to both (Japan's #1 partner at 22%, Singapore's largest non-oil partner), so China hard-landing is approximately neutral for the cross.
key_bilateral_metrics: Iran resolution probability: 40% peace / 35% frozen / 25% re-escalation
US-Japan auto tariff risk: ~18% probability
China demand risk: roughly symmetrical for both economies
Taiwan-China tail risk: modest, affects both similarly
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price benign outcomes; the bilateral asymmetry is small enough to warrant Neutral.

factors.horizon_split: Both Neutral at similar confidence; event risk is bilateral rather than pair-directional.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Singapore's fiscal and sovereign profile is unambiguously superior to Japan's. Singapore: AAA (all three agencies), structural surplus, debt-for-investment-not-deficit, GIC/Temasek combined buffer SGD 2T. Japan: A+/A1/A, 250% gross debt/GDP, BOJ holds ~50% of JGBs, long-end yields at multi-decade highs (30Y ~3.85%, 40Y 4.21%). The sovereign-credit gap is meaningful for capital allocation — SGS are AAA-Asia paper with a scarcity premium, while JGBs face rising supply pressure from Takaichi's fiscal stimulus. However, the tactical marginal signal is small because the AAA premium is structurally embedded in SGD pricing and Japan's fiscal risk is chronic rather than acute.
key_bilateral_metrics: Singapore rating: AAA (all three) vs Japan: A+/A1/A
Singapore net asset position: deeply positive vs Japan debt/GDP: ~250%
SGS 10Y: 2.10% vs JGB 10Y: 2.50% (Japan yields higher despite lower rating)
Japan JGB long-end stress: 30Y ~3.85%, 40Y 4.21% (record-area)
Singapore FY2026 budget: modest Iran-war package (S$1bn)
tactical.what_is_priced: The AAA premium is fully embedded. No marginal tactical signal.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the sovereign risk differential provides a steady tailwind for SGD/JPY, particularly if JGB long-end yields continue rising and create a fiscal-constraint narrative that limits BOJ normalisation pace. Each 100bp rise in average JGB yield costs Japan ~2% of GDP in additional debt servicing — a potential second-order constraint on BOJ hawkishness that would be mildly SGD/JPY-bullish.
key_bilateral_metrics: Japan interest expense as % of revenue: ~22% (rising)
Singapore fiscal position: structural surplus, no debt sustainability concern
JGB-BOJ circular dynamic: BOJ holds ~50% of outstanding JGBs
Fiscal-constraint risk to BOJ normalisation: non-trivial tail
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price Japan fiscal sustainability as benign on BOJ-buyer assumption. The tail risk is under-priced.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bullish with high confidence; the sovereign differential is a permanent structural positive for SGD/JPY.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Spot at 123.70 trades below the 50d SMA (124.37) but above the 100d SMA (123.68) and 200d SMA (121.91). The immediate MA configuration suggests consolidation: the pullback from the 125.47 high has brought spot back to the 100d SMA, which is acting as support. The 50d SMA overhead is the first resistance hurdle. The RSI (14) at approximately 59.66 suggests neutral conditions, while the MACD reading of 0.4567 indicates a buy signal. The pair is in a consolidation regime after the sharp 30 April correction, with neither momentum nor trend signals providing a clear directional edge. Support: 122.80 (Fibonacci 0.786 / order block), 121.91 (200d SMA), 120.50 (structural). Resistance: 124.37 (50d SMA), 125.00–125.50 (52-week high zone).
key_bilateral_metrics: Spot: 123.70 (below 50d SMA 124.37, above 100d SMA 123.68)
200d SMA: 121.91 (strategic support)
52-week range: 107.08–125.47 (90th percentile)
RSI (14): ~60 (neutral)
Daily ATR: ~0.80 yen (~65 pips)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing consolidation around the 122–125 range. Options on this cross are thinly traded; the vol landscape is dominated by the USD/JPY leg.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic picture is one of a mature uptrend approaching exhaustion. The pair has rallied ~17% from the April 2025 low, and the pullback from 125.47 may be the beginning of a multi-month correction as BOJ normalisation compresses the pair. If the 200d SMA at 121.91 is tested and broken, it would confirm a trend reversal and trigger CTA selling. The 52-week range percentile at 90 means spot is extended on a statistical basis. XS.com's base case sees SGD/JPY likely to trade between 113.00 and 118.20 in 2026 — well below current spot — reflecting a structurally bearish view driven by BOJ normalisation. However, the actual price trajectory has already exceeded this forecast significantly, suggesting the base case may underestimate MAS-driven SGD strength. CoinCodex forecasts a 2026 range of 123.02 to 135.84 with an average of 128.02, representing a more bullish take.
key_bilateral_metrics: 200d SMA: 121.91 (key strategic support / potential reversal level)
52-week range percentile: 90 (extended)
CoinCodex 2026 range: 123–136 (bullish)
XS.com 2026 range: 113–118 (bearish, pre-dated actual rally)
30rates.com end-June 2026: 123; end-Dec 2026: 133
strategic.what_is_priced: Forecasters are divided. The pair sits near the upper bound of fundamentally-anchored models but within range of momentum-based forecasts. The variance itself suggests a turning point.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (55) reflecting range-bound consolidation; Strategic Bearish (55) reflecting the mature uptrend and BOJ normalisation headwind.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: SGD/JPY has no standalone listed options market and is a thin OTC cross. Volatility is estimated at ~6–8% annualised from OHLC ranges; the vol landscape is dominated by the USD/JPY leg (~9.5% 1M IV). Event risk through the June BOJ and FOMC meetings creates modest front-end vol premium. Daily ATR ~0.80 yen (~65 pips).
expression_assessment.directional_expression: Given the thin OTC options market for SGD/JPY, the directional view is best expressed via spot or through the component legs. For a bearish SGD/JPY view, selling SGD/JPY spot with a stop above 125.50 is the cleanest expression. Alternatively, constructing the position through EUR/JPY puts (liquid) and adjusting the SGD exposure via USD/SGD offers better liquidity execution.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The June 17 BOJ MPM is the primary event timing consideration — do not enter a strategic short without accounting for the BOJ binary. If shorting ahead of June, size should be reduced to allow for a 200–300 pip adverse move on a BOJ hold. Post-June-confirmation offers cleaner entry.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: SGD/JPY is a secondary FX cross with limited liquidity versus primary USD pairs. Bid-offer spreads widen during Asian off-hours. For larger positions, execution via the component legs (USD/JPY + USD/SGD) is preferable to direct SGD/JPY execution to minimise market impact.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Non-USD cross; USD liquidity factor reduced by −0.05 on both horizons as USD funding is a less direct driver for SGD/JPY

tactical.bilateral_analysis: For this cross pair, USD liquidity transmits through the vehicle currency channel — most SGD/JPY transactions settle via two USD legs (buy SGD/sell USD + buy USD/sell JPY). Fed at 3.50–3.75% with dovish bias (8-4 split at April FOMC) provides stable USD funding. The USD-JPY and USD-SGD cross-currency bases are structurally different: JPY/USD basis is persistently negative (Japanese institutions pay premium for USD), while SGD/USD basis is near-flat (Singapore's banking system is well-funded). This asymmetry marginally favours SGD/JPY upside through the funding-cost channel — it costs Japanese institutions more to hold USD than Singapore institutions, creating a subtle flow pressure. However, the magnitude is too small to generate tactical signal.
key_bilateral_metrics: Fed funds: 3.50–3.75% (held, dovish bias)
JPY/USD 3M basis: ~−30 to −50 bps (structural negative)
SGD/USD 3M basis: ~0 to −5 bps (near-flat)
Dollar smile position: mild-Goldilocks middle (mildly USD-bearish)
tactical.what_is_priced: Benign USD funding for both legs. No bilateral edge from this factor tactically.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the Warsh-Fed transition likely delivers easier USD policy (one cut by Q4 2026). As USD funding eases, the JPY/USD basis compresses (less negative), reducing the cost of JPY-funded positions — this is JPY-bullish through the funding channel and therefore SGD/JPY-bearish. Singapore's banking system (DBS, OCBC, UOB) benefits from easier USD funding too, but the magnitude is smaller because SGD funding stress is already minimal. The net effect of USD liquidity easing is mildly SGD/JPY-bearish through the differential funding-cost channel.
key_bilateral_metrics: Projected Fed funds end-2026: 3.25–3.50% (one cut)
JPY/USD basis improvement: expected −30bp → −15bp
SGD/USD basis: stable near zero
Net funding-cost differential change: ~15bp in JPY's favour
Warsh transition: dovish bias confirmed
strategic.what_is_priced: One Fed cut is priced; the funding-cost improvement for JPY is partially priced through JPY forwards but not fully in SGD/JPY cross.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (55) reflecting benign conditions; Strategic Bearish (55) reflecting the differential funding-cost improvement for JPY as Fed eases.

adjustments.reason: SGD/JPY has moderate risk-proxy sensitivity through the JPY safe-haven leg; boost by +0.03 tactical and +0.02 strategic
Oil-price differential is the dominant bilateral factor for this pair; boost by +0.02 both horizons (tactical: 0.06→0.08, strategic: 0.16→0.18)
Non-USD cross; USD liquidity is a less direct driver; reduce by −0.05 both horizons (tactical: 0.11→0.06, strategic: 0.12→0.07)

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score +0.195 maps to a very low-conviction bullish signal, at the border of Neutral and Conviction 2. The score reflects the dominance of Neutral factors (6 of 10) with modest bullish contributions from Inflation/Growth, External Balance, and Fiscal/Sovereign. No factors are bearish tactically, reflecting a pair that is supported but lacks strong directional thrust.
tactical.rationale: The tactical picture for SGD/JPY is one of consolidation at elevated levels with a modest upward bias. The pair's near-term direction is dominated by the 2–4 week data vacuum before the BOJ June 17 meeting, which is the single most important bilateral catalyst. The three bullish factors — Singapore's growth advantage (4.6% vs 0.5%), the oil-related terms-of-trade differential (SGD benefits more from current $102 Brent through refining margins), and the sovereign-credit differential (AAA vs A+) — provide a floor under the pair. However, the high-weighted Policy & Rates factor (24%) is Neutral because both central banks are in tightening mode with approximately equal surprise potential in the immediate window. The interaction of the two dossier views creates a muted cross-rate signal: both dossiers are bullish their respective currencies vs USD, which is a conflict for the cross. Technically, the pair sits in a consolidation zone — below the 50d SMA (124.37) and above the 100d SMA (123.68), with RSI neutral at ~60 and MACD marginally positive. The path of least resistance is sideways (122.50–124.50) with a mild upward drift toward the 50d SMA at 124.37, absent a BOJ June surprise. Key catalysts within the window: Singapore April CPI (25 May, upside risk to 2.1%), BOJ Summary of Opinions (~7 May released), Iran ceasefire developments. Conviction is low at 2/5 because the tactical edge is small — the net weighted score of +0.195 is barely above zero — and the BOJ June binary (35–40% hike probability) creates significant two-way risk that could move the pair 200–300 pips in either direction. The weight-adjusted score also reflects the reduced importance of the USD Liquidity factor (reduced to 0.06) and the boosted External Balance factor (increased to 0.08), which captures the oil-asymmetry channel as a genuine bilateral differentiator for this pair.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: SGD/JPY ranks as a below-average opportunity among JPY crosses. Within all JPY shorts (long XXX/JPY), USD/JPY is the most attractive (widest rate gap, most liquid), followed by AUD/JPY (highest carry at ~360 bps), GBP/JPY (highest carry-to-vol), and then SGD/JPY (lowest carry at +40 bps, thinnest liquidity). Among SGD crosses, long SGD/JPY is less attractive than short USD/SGD (direct MAS policy expression) or short AUD/SGD (captures Asia relative value). SGD/JPY is a secondary expression, not a primary trade.

conditions: BOJ Summary of Opinions mildly hawkish but not aggressively so
base_case.path: Pair oscillates around the 123.68 100d SMA, testing 124.37 50d SMA as resistance and 122.80 Fibonacci support on dips. Volatility modest (~0.80 yen daily ATR). Pair drifts toward 124 by late May on firm Singapore CPI, then consolidates into June BOJ event.

conditions: Singapore April CPI prints 2.1%+ (MAS July tightening odds rise above 40%)
BOJ Summary of Opinions softens hawkish dissent narrative (fewer than 3 members at 1.00%)
Iran ceasefire formalised (Brent dips but SGD refining margin premium maintained)
BOJ June hike surprise leaked via Summary of Opinions rhetoric (4+ members at 1.00%)
Iran re-escalation drives Brent to $115+ (JPY safe-haven bid overwhelms oil asymmetry)
Risk-off episode (VIX spikes above 25) activates JPY repatriation flows

tactical.path_expectation: The 2–4 week path is range-bound with a mild upward bias: spot oscillates around the 123.68 100d SMA, testing the 124.37 50d SMA as resistance and 122.80 (Fibonacci 0.786/order block support) on dips. Volatility is modest (~0.80 yen daily ATR) with episodic spikes on Iran headlines. The pair drifts toward 124 by late May if the Singapore April CPI print is firm (≥2.0%), reinforcing the MAS hawkish narrative. After the CPI release, the pair consolidates in a tighter 123.50–124.50 range into the June BOJ event, with the market reluctant to push toward 125 given the binary hike risk. Any break above 125.00 on a technical basis would trigger re-evaluation of the upside scenario and would require reassessment of the BOJ June probability. On the downside, 122.80 (Fibonacci/order block) is the key near-term support — a daily close below this level would signal the downside scenario is unfolding.

increase_conditions: Singapore April CPI ≥2.0% AND Brent stays $100+ AND BOJ Summary is not overwhelmingly hawkish (fewer than 4 members at 1.00%): increase to 3
BOJ June hold confirmed by pre-meeting leaks AND MAS signals July tightening simultaneously: increase to 4
decrease_conditions: BOJ Summary reveals 4+ members supporting June hike AND Brent falls below $90: decrease to 1
BOJ surprises with June hike to 1.00% (pre-meeting): flip to Bearish / decrease to 0

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Slightly positive expected value but weak asymmetry. The distribution is negatively skewed — the downside scenario involves a larger move (−220 pips) than the upside (+230 pips) and both tail scenarios are equally probable. Not a high-conviction tactical setup; position sizing should be conservative.

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score −0.294 maps to Conviction 2 Bearish (in the −0.20 to −0.40 range). Five factors are bearish, one is bullish (Fiscal/Sovereign), and four are neutral. The dominant bearish drivers are Policy & Rates (−0.144) and Guidance & Credibility (−0.072), reflecting the BOJ normalisation thesis. The sole bullish offset from Fiscal/Sovereign (+0.046) is insufficient to counter the directional weight.
strategic.rationale: The strategic case for SGD/JPY is moderately bearish, driven by the BOJ normalisation path converting the pair from a positive-carry to a negative-carry expression. The rate-differential trajectory is the decisive factor: BOJ projected at 1.00–1.25% by year-end while SORA drifts lower toward ~1.00% implies the differential flips from +40 bps to −10 to −110 bps — a regime shift that historically correlates with SGD/JPY depreciation. The BOJ guidance framework (2.8% FY2026 CPI, three dissenters already at 1.00%) provides a credible anchor for two hikes by year-end, against which MAS's guidance is less surprising (slope already steepened, no further tightening base case). JPY's structural undervaluation (34% below PPP vs SGD) and safe-haven re-assertion as oil normalises add bearish bias. The sole bullish factor — Fiscal/Sovereign — reflects Singapore's AAA advantage but its 7% weight is insufficient to counter the 24% Policy & Rates bearish contribution. The interaction of the two dossiers is the key bilateral insight: both currencies are independently bullish vs USD, but on the cross, JPY's rate-normalisation story is catching up to SGD's structural advantages, compressing the pair. J.P. Morgan Private Bank has a structurally bullish outlook on the yen, expecting repatriation flows by Japanese investors and converging interest rate differentials to further support USD/JPY downside — this JPY bullishness directly transmits into SGD/JPY bearishness. The 10Y yield inversion (SGS 2.10% < JGB 2.50%) is a bond-market leading indicator confirming this view. Strategically, the External Balance factor turns neutral as Brent normalises toward $80–90, removing the tactical oil-asymmetry tailwind and allowing the rate-differential trajectory to dominate. Target: SGD/JPY 119–122 by November 2026, based on USD/JPY 150–153 and USD/SGD 1.24–1.25 (consensus-derived). The conviction of 2/5 reflects genuine uncertainty about the pace and number of BOJ hikes — the strategic bearish thesis is BOJ-delivery-dependent.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: SGD/JPY short ranks as a moderate-priority trade among JPY longs. AUD/JPY short is more attractive (higher carry give-up = bigger payoff on rate differential compression, stronger risk-off beta). EUR/JPY short is the cleanest expression (ECB cutting into BOJ hiking, widest policy divergence). SGD/JPY short ranks 3rd–4th among JPY crosses due to the offsetting SGD structural strength and the AAA fiscal anchor, making the downside more gradual and less convex than other JPY crosses.

conditions: BOJ delivers one hike (June or July) to 1.00%
Fed cuts once by Q4 2026 (Warsh dovish pivot)
base_case.path: Phase 1 (May–June): consolidation 122–125, ending with BOJ June resolution. Phase 2 (July–September): directional move lower as BOJ hike compresses rate differential — target 120–122. Phase 3 (October–November): possible acceleration through 120 if second BOJ hike or Fed cut materialises. Stair-step lower with brief consolidation plateaus at each MA level.

conditions: BOJ holds throughout 2026 (dovish disappointment — JPY longs capitulate)
Iran ceasefire fails (oil $115 sustained, SGD/JPY oil-asymmetry extends)
BOJ delivers two hikes to 1.25% (June and October/November)
Fed cuts 50bp+ (Warsh accelerated easing) and Iran ceasefire firms (Brent <$80)
JPY safe-haven bid activates on risk-off episode (VIX >30)

strategic.path_expectation: The 1–6 month path is a phased decline with three distinct phases. Phase 1 (May–June): consolidation in the 122–125 range as the market awaits the BOJ June 17 decision; the pair trades on inertia and oil-asymmetry support near 123–124. Phase 2 (July–September): directional move lower following BOJ hike delivery — the rate differential compression to near-zero or negative triggers systematic selling; target 120–122 with the 200d SMA at 121.91 as the key inflection point. Phase 3 (October–November): potential acceleration below 120 if a second BOJ hike materialises or the Fed cuts, widening the interest rate divergence; target 119–120 in the base case. Pullback levels on any bounce: 124 then 125 in Phase 1; 122 then 123 in Phase 2. The pattern shape is stair-step lower with brief consolidation plateaus at each MA level, consistent with a carry-unwind rather than a panic-driven collapse.

increase_conditions: BOJ delivers June hike to 1.00% AND Brent falls below $90 (removes SGD/JPY oil tailwind): increase to 3 Bearish
BOJ delivers two hikes by Q3 AND Fed cuts AND SGD/JPY breaks below 120: increase to 4 Bearish
decrease_conditions: BOJ holds through September AND Brent re-spikes to $115 (oil asymmetry extends SGD/JPY rally): decrease to 1 Bearish
BOJ explicitly reverses normalisation AND MAS further tightens at July AND October: flip to Bullish

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Modestly positive expected value; the higher downside-case probability (30% vs 20% upside) improves the expected return on a short position. The distribution is positively skewed for the bear thesis — the larger downside-case payoff (470 pips) and higher probability create a slight edge despite the roughly symmetric single-scenario risk/reward.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: No direct bank consensus for SGD/JPY found. Derived from component forecasts: Bloomberg consensus USD/JPY end-2026: 153, USD/SGD ~1.25 implies SGD/JPY ~122. CoinCodex forecasts SGD/JPY averaging 128.02 in 2026 (range 123.02–135.84). 30rates.com projects SGD/JPY ending May at 123 and June at 123. LongForecast sees SGD/JPY rising to 131 by end-June 2026. XS.com projects 113–118 range for 2026.
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: below consensus (for strategic view vs momentum-based forecasters)
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: 100–400 pips below algorithmic forecasters such as CoinCodex (128) and LongForecast (131); broadly aligned with bank-derived consensus (~122)
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We weight the BOJ's 6-3 hawkish vote and 2.8% FY2026 core CPI projection more heavily than consensus algorithmic forecasts, and we explicitly model the MAS-BOJ rate differential flipping negative by Q4 2026 — a qualitative regime shift from positive-carry to negative-carry pair that model-based extrapolation misses. Momentum-based forecasters (CoinCodex, LongForecast) project the recent 17% uptrend continuation without incorporating the fundamental carry-regime change. Our strategic bearish view diverges from these models because we assign 50–60% probability to the BOJ two-hike scenario versus the near-zero probability embedded in simple trend-following models.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: +235 pips expected value on strategic short; base case +320 pips from 123.70 to 120.50
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: −330 pips if BOJ disappoints and SGD/JPY rallies to 127 on consensus positioning (LongForecast/CoinCodex scenario)

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: The tactical and strategic stances diverge because the BOJ June 17 MPM is the inflection point separating the two horizons. The tactical window (2–4 weeks) predates this event, so the pair holds elevated levels on inertia and oil-related terms-of-trade advantage, producing a mildly bullish signal. The strategic window (1–6 months) encompasses 2–3 BOJ MPMs where hike delivery would structurally compress the rate differential and convert the pair to negative carry, producing a bearish signal. This is a classic pre-catalyst neutral / post-catalyst directional divergence.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: BOJ June 17 MPM — a hike to 1.00% triggers the strategic bearish path; a hold extends the tactical neutral-to-bullish window into the July BOJ decision
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Do not short SGD/JPY into the June event with a 2–4 week horizon; instead, wait for the June outcome and position for Phase 2 (post-hike correction) or Phase 1 continuation (if hold). The divergent stance creates an opportunity to build a strategic short after the June catalyst confirms direction. Size tactically conservative to allow for the binary event.

forward_vs_spot.forward_1m: ~123.67 (flat to slightly lower, reflecting minimal +40 bps rate differential, negligible forward depreciation)
forward_vs_spot.expected_rate_6m: ~120.50 (base case: post-hike compression to Phase 2 target)
forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: Forward implies 123.60; we expect 122.50 = ~110 pip edge on short SGD/JPY at 3M
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: Forward implies 123.45; we expect 120.50 = ~295 pip edge on short SGD/JPY at 6M (~2.4% alpha opportunity)

catalysts.rationale: First full-month BOP data reflecting Iran-war energy import surge. A weaker goods balance confirms Japan's terms-of-trade damage but elevated primary income provides offset. Net effect on SGD/JPY is modest and ambiguous — goods deficit is JPY-bearish but primary income surplus is structural JPY-bullish. The data will inform the BOJ's trade balance assessment ahead of the June MPM.

Weak GDP could delay BOJ hike, which would be SGD/JPY-bullish by removing rate normalisation pressure. However, the BOJ has already cut FY2026 GDP to 0.5% — a weak print is largely priced. The BOJ has signalled it will look through transitory growth weakness to focus on wage-inflation dynamics. Only a sharply negative print (negative GDP) would materially delay the June hike decision.

Tokyo CPI is the leading indicator for national Japan CPI and the most timely inflation signal for the BOJ. A print above 2.0% would raise June BOJ hike probability from ~35–40% to ~50%, compressing SGD/JPY by an estimated 50–100 pips as JPY strengthens. Consensus is ~1.5–1.7%; an upside surprise to 2.0%+ would be the most impactful near-term JPY catalyst and the key bilateral data point before the June BOJ meeting.

Singapore CPI for April is the primary SGD catalyst in the near-term window. Consensus 1.9–2.0% headline; an upside surprise to 2.1%+ would lift MAS July tightening odds above 40%, supporting SGD and pushing SGD/JPY higher by ~50–80 pips. A downside miss below 1.7% would reduce MAS hawkish credibility and weigh on SGD, exerting mild SGD/JPY-bearish pressure. The bilateral impact is asymmetric: the upside SGD surprise matters more than the downside for the cross.

The single most important catalyst for SGD/JPY. A BOJ hike to 1.00% with a 5-4 or 6-3 vote would drive SGD/JPY 200–300 pips lower immediately as the rate differential compresses to near-zero or negative. A dovish hold would maintain the 122–125 range and extend the tactical consolidation phase, lifting the pair toward 124–125. The binary nature of this event means the pair trades in a compressed range until the outcome is known. The BOJ hike probability is ~35–40%, meaning the hold is still the base case, but the tail risk of the hike scenario generates downside risk exposure throughout the preceding weeks.

Fed hold is widely expected at the June FOMC meeting. A dovish lean is broadly USD-bearish, which transmits roughly equally through both the SGD and JPY legs of this cross via the USD vehicle currency. The net impact on SGD/JPY from a Fed hold or mild dovish lean is approximately zero. Only an unexpected cut or a sharply hawkish hold (stressing USD/JPY higher) would meaningfully shift the SGD/JPY cross via the USD channel.

catalysts.event: MAS July 2026 Quarterly Monetary Policy Statement
catalysts.rationale: A further MAS slope steepening at the July MPS (~25% probability) would lift SGD/JPY by ~50–100 pips by widening the SGD nominal appreciation path. If both the June BOJ hold and July MAS tightening occur simultaneously, the rate differential would widen in SGD's favour, potentially reversing the strategic bearish thesis. The base case is MAS hold at July; any tightening would be a positive SGD surprise and a key invalidator for the strategic bearish SGD/JPY view.

If June was a hold, July becomes the primary candidate for BOJ hike delivery. The July meeting includes the Outlook Report, giving the BOJ an opportunity to revise its inflation and GDP projections and deliver a hike with full communication support. A July hike to 1.00% would compress SGD/JPY toward 120 as the rate differential turns negative. If June was already a hike, the July meeting would be assessed for second-hike signals, with JPY-bullish implications for the pair.

invalidators.condition: BOJ holds through September 2026 without hiking AND hawkish dissent count falls to 1 or fewer board members
invalidators.mechanism: The rate-normalisation thesis is the primary driver of the strategic SGD/JPY bearish view. If BOJ fails to deliver by September and dissent wanes, the carry regime stays positive, JPY safe-haven repatriation channel remains dormant, and SGD's structural advantages (AAA, 17.5% C/A, MAS tightening) reassert as the dominant pair drivers, supporting SGD/JPY.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BOJ MPM vote splits and Summary of Opinions

invalidators.condition: Brent crude sustains above $120/bbl for 10+ consecutive trading days
invalidators.new_stance: Bullish — oil-asymmetry channel overwhelms rate-differential compression
invalidators.mechanism: Japan's 95.9% Middle East crude dependence means a sustained Brent above $120 would severely damage Japan's terms of trade, overwhelming any rate-normalisation JPY benefit. Singapore's refining margins would expand further. The oil-asymmetry bilateral driver would dominate, pushing SGD/JPY higher. This scenario is most likely from an Iran re-escalation or OPEC+ supply shock.

invalidators.condition: MAS further tightens at July 2026 MPS (slope steepened again) AND BOJ holds at June MPM
invalidators.new_stance: Bullish — rate differential widens in SGD's favour
invalidators.mechanism: A simultaneous MAS tightening and BOJ hold would be the best-case outcome for SGD/JPY bulls — MAS slope steepening increases SGD appreciation, while BOJ hold keeps JPY depressed and the carry positive. The pair would likely break above 125.50 and test 126–127.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: MAS MPS announcement (July 2026) and BOJ June MPM outcome

invalidators.condition: SGD/JPY breaks above 126.50 with sustained volume (daily close above for 3 consecutive days)
invalidators.new_stance: Bullish — technical breakout invalidates consolidation-into-decline thesis
invalidators.mechanism: A sustained break above 126.50 would represent a new 52-week high above the April 2026 peak (125.47), invalidating the technical picture of a mature uptrend approaching exhaustion. CTA momentum strategies would turn long, and the BOJ normalisation thesis would require reassessment. This scenario requires either a BOJ hold surprise or a sharp MAS tightening.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SGD/JPY daily closing price and volume on TradingView

invalidators.condition: Japan national core CPI falls below 1.5% for two or more consecutive monthly prints
invalidators.mechanism: If Japan's inflation falls back below 1.5%, the BOJ's 2.8% FY2026 CPI projection would look increasingly unrealistic, delaying or cancelling the hike path. The three-dissenter June hike scenario would become untenable. The strategic bearish thesis rests on BOJ delivery — without the inflation anchor, normalisation is off the table and SGD/JPY reverts to trading on structural fundamentals where SGD has the advantage.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Japan national CPI (monthly release from Statistics Bureau)

signposts.confirms_if: Rises to 1.00% by Q3 2026 (June or July MPM delivery)
signposts.invalidates_if: Remains at 0.75% through September 2026 (no hike by third consecutive meeting opportunity)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The dominant driver of the SGD/JPY strategic thesis. A hike to 1.00% flips the carry from +40 bps to −25 bps, converting SGD/JPY from a positive-carry to negative-carry pair for the first time — a regime shift that historically drives structural position unwinding and pair depreciation of 200–500 pips.

signposts.confirms_if: Falls below $90 sustained for 5+ trading days (removes SGD/JPY bullish ToT bias, allows rate-differential thesis to dominate)
signposts.invalidates_if: Rises above $120 sustained for 10+ trading days (re-establishes SGD/JPY bullish ToT bias through oil-asymmetry channel)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The key bilateral commodity exposure variable for this pair. Japan's 95.9% Middle East crude dependence versus Singapore's refining-offset structure creates the most powerful bilateral terms-of-trade asymmetry in this cross. Brent is the single commodity that matters most for SGD/JPY direction, particularly when the rate differential is small.

signposts.confirms_if: Spread widens to −60 bps or more (JGB yields rising faster than SGS, confirming BOJ normalisation dominance)
signposts.invalidates_if: Spread narrows to 0 or turns positive (SGS yields catch up to JGB, suggesting MAS hawkishness is re-pricing or BOJ is stalling)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 10Y sovereign yield spread is a leading indicator for SGD/JPY direction. Bond markets typically lead FX by 3–6 months on rate-differential repricing. The current SGS < JGB inversion is historically unusual given Singapore's AAA rating and is the bond market's forward statement that BOJ normalisation will dominate the SGD/JPY bilateral story in 2026.

signposts.indicator: CFTC JPY Leveraged Funds Net Position
signposts.confirms_if: Sustained net long 40,000–80,000 contracts with weekly stability (durable JPY demand supporting the bearish SGD/JPY view through repatriation)
signposts.invalidates_if: Reverts to net short −30,000 contracts (JPY thesis losing momentum, BOJ disappointment unwind — would be SGD/JPY-bullish)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The only observable positioning proxy for one leg of this pair (SGD has zero CFTC data). JPY positioning has been the key surprise driver in 2025–2026; the shift from structural short to net long represents a secular change in JPY-cross dynamics. A sustained long confirms the BOJ normalisation narrative; an unwind would signal BOJ disappointment and trigger SGD/JPY upside.
signposts.frequency: Weekly (released every Friday for prior Tuesday)

signposts.confirms_if: Moderates to +5–8% y/y (reducing Singapore's growth premium over Japan, narrowing the bilateral growth differential that supports SGD)
signposts.invalidates_if: Accelerates above +20% y/y (extending Singapore's AI-electronics export advantage and widening the bilateral growth differential — SGD/JPY-bullish)
signposts.bilateral_importance: NODX measures the durability of Singapore's primary tactical advantage over Japan in this cross — export growth driven by the AI-electronics boom. The sustainability of +74% y/y electronics growth is the key uncertainty in the tactical bullish SGD assessment. A deceleration toward trend removes the growth-differential support for SGD, allowing the rate-normalisation thesis to dominate uncontested.

assumptions: Pair volatility estimation: SGD/JPY has no standalone listed options market. Volatility estimated from daily OHLC ranges (~6–8% annualised) and inferred from component USD/SGD (~5% 1M IV) and USD/JPY (~9.5% 1M IV) implieds using the triangulation formula with estimated correlation (~0.3–0.5).

Forward points: Estimated from the tiny MAS SORA–BOJ policy rate differential (+40 bps). Precise FX forward quotes for SGD/JPY not retrieved; inferred from component USD/SGD and USD/JPY forwards. The resulting forward curve is approximately flat to slightly negative for SGD/JPY at all tenors.

Consensus forecasts: Algorithmic forecasters (CoinCodex, 30rates, LongForecast, WalletInvestor) have wide dispersion (range 113–136 for 2026), reflecting the pair's sensitivity to the binary BOJ hike outcome. Bank-specific SGD/JPY forecasts from major institutions were not found; consensus is derived from individual USD/JPY and USD/SGD bank forecasts.

Weight adjustments: Risk Appetite boosted (+0.03 tactical, +0.02 strategic) to reflect the JPY safe-haven leg; External Balance boosted (+0.02 both horizons) for the oil-asymmetry bilateral driver; USD Liquidity reduced (−0.05 both horizons) for non-USD cross. These adjustments are modest and sum-preserving within each horizon.

The strategic bearish SGD/JPY thesis rests primarily on BOJ delivery. If the BOJ disappoints through September 2026, the thesis is invalid and the pair reverts to trading on Singapore's structural advantages (AAA, C/A surplus, MAS credibility), which are bullish for the cross.

Bilateral trade data: Singapore imports from Japan ~$22bn and Japan exports to Singapore ~$20bn (2024) indicate roughly balanced flows with no dominant FX conversion pressure. Investment flows are bidirectional but asymmetric, with Japanese corporate FDI into Singapore historically larger than reverse flows.

CFTC SGD futures data is effectively uninformative (zero open interest). Positioning analysis for the SGD leg is inferred from MAS reserve movements, flow reports, and anecdotal family-office evidence rather than quantitative positioning metrics.

The tactical net stance is labelled Neutral-to-Mildly-Bullish but formally mapped to Neutral (conviction 2) in the JSON schema, reflecting the weak positive score (+0.195) that sits at the boundary between Neutral and low-conviction Bullish.
